title: "Connect Laptop to LCD tv with VGA"

### Post by Vaelrith on 2008-04-14
I am trying to connect my HP laptop to my lcd tv to use as an external monitor.  I am connecting VGA to VGA.  When I turn the computer on I can see grub, then the Ubuntu loading screen, but as soon as it finishes starting up the tv says "No signal".  The laptop screen still works fine.  I have a nVidia graphics card and here is my xorg.conf


### Post by sillyxone on 2008-04-14
at the very least, try using the monitor, set it to lowest resolution (800x600 or 640x480) then plug the LCD and reboot. I'm saying this because a 720p TV has maximum resolution of 1368x768, and thus will say "no signal" if it sees unsupported modes.

if you want to switch on the fly, try xrandr when unplug/plug the LCD:

```
xrandr --output VGA --auto
```

EDITED: I didn't realize you are using a laptop, the internal LCD is referred as LVDS, and external LCD is called VGA. You can run xrandr without parameter to list the detected monitors. So for example, if you want to have both on:

```
xrandr --output LVDS --auto --output VGA --auto
```

---

### Post by Vaelrith on 2008-04-14
The last solution worked at first.  I restarted my computer and everything worked fine except for my computer started up in safe graphics mode and I had to uninstall then reinstall the nvidia driver to get it back.  Doing that caused me to go back to the same problem as before.  Is there a way I can set the resolution of the VGA different from the resolution of the LVDS while both are in use?

For example, my laptop screen resolution is 1440x900 is there a way to make the the VGA show up in the correct resolution for a 720p tv?

Or, is there a way for the resolution to change when I plug in the VGA, then change back to the correct resolution for the LVDS?

Thanks for the help.

---

### Post by sillyxone on 2008-04-14
as mentioned, 720p TV cannot display 1440x900 since its maximum resolution is 1368x768.

Usually I only connect to external monitor on demand so xrandr is fine for me. The workflow is somewhat like this:

1) Boot up without TV plugged in, internal LCD at 1440x900
2) Connect TV/Projector, run "xrandr --output LVDS --auto --output VGA --auto". This will set both internal and external monitors to highest common resolution, which is 1368x768
3) When done with the TV/Projector, optionally unplug the external monitor, run "xrandr --output LVDS --auto --output VGA --off". This will turn off the external monitor and return internal LCD back to 1440x900. You can repeat 2-3 without rebooting.

I have Intel onboard so I don't know about Nvidia, perhaps you can search for xinorama or dual-head display settings specifically for Nvidia driver. The shortcut key (Fn-F8 on my Dell D620) works inconsistently so I rely on xrandr most of the time. Read the manual of xrandr for more options. 

About the problem of booting into safe graphic mode, I think I've seen it before. It could be that if you have the external monitor plugged in on boot up, the video driver will automatically detect it and in many cases, set both monitors to weird resolutions. Also, your xorg.conf file doesn't list 1440x900 but 1400x1050 instead, could it be the culprit?

---

### Post by Vaelrith on 2008-04-15
Still having some trouble...

After reinstalling the graphics driver, it seems like the xorg.conf may have changed a little.  Here is the updated version: 

```
# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"	"ImPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"	"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Synaptics Touchpad"
	Driver		"synaptics"
	Option		"SendCoreEvents"	"true"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/psaux"
	Option		"Protocol"	"auto-dev"
	Option		"HorizEdgeScroll"	"0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"stylus"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"nVidia Corporation C51 [Geforce 6150 Go]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	Busid		"PCI:0:5:0"
	Option		"AddARGBVisuals"	"True"
	Option		"AddARGBGLXVisuals"	"True"
	Option		"NoLogo"	"True"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Generic Monitor"
	Option		"DPMS"
	Horizsync	28-72
	Vertrefresh	43-60
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"nVidia Corporation C51 [Geforce 6150 Go]"
	Monitor		"Generic Monitor"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1440x900"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen "Default Screen"
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	
	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection
Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
EndSection
```



> 2) Connect TV/Projector, run "xrandr --output LVDS --auto --output VGA --auto". This will set both internal and external monitors to highest common resolution, which is 1368x768

EDIT:  I played with "sudo nvidia-settings" and got it to work on the tv, the only problem is it doesn't replicate the computer screen onto the tv.  If I set it to "Seperate X Screen" it just makes it like I have 2 desktops which are controlled independently, except the desktop on the tv doesn't have window borders and it looks like the default desktop after first installing ubuntu.  If I do twinview, that just makes it like 2 monitors next to each other sharing a single desktop.  Also the tv shows the desktop pushed to the right and there is a lot of blank black space on the left; the desktop doesn't fill the entirety of the tv.

Making progress, if this is the best then I guess I can live with it, but it would be nice to be able to replicate the laptop screen onto the entirety of the tv screen.

Thanks again
